coredata.dc:title: Experimental study on the radiative heat flux from flaming 18650- and 26650-type LFP battery cells
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2026 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Lugaresi, Francesca
Bugryniec, Peter
Sadeghi, Hosein
Williams, Daniel
Wootton, Mark
Brown, Solomon
Restuccia, Francesco
coredata.dc:description:
Fires from lithium-ion cells are a major heat transfer pathway, promoting thermal runaway propagation and spreading flames to surrounding cells. This study presents new experimental insights into the fire behaviour of lithium iron phosphate (LFP) cells by quantifying radiative heat flux, gas temperature, and cell surface temperature during venting and flaming. The key contributions are: (i) the first spatially and time-resolved measurements of radiative heat flux from single-cell LFP fires, revealing non-uniform radiation with peak intensities varying up to
∼ 10 ×
across the horizontal plane; and (ii) a controlled comparison of 18650 and 26650 cylindrical cells, demonstrating fundamental differences in combustion behaviour and peak radiative output. Fully charged cells were heated under constant power to induce thermal runaway, with and without an external ignition source. Three gas emission stages were identified: (1) steady emission post-venting, (2) increased gas production with self-heating, and (3) rapid gas release at thermal runaway. Forced ignition sustained flames in stages 1 and 3 for 18650 cells, whereas 26650 cells maintained combustion throughout. Peak radiative heat fluxes reached ∼ 150 kW/m
2
for 18650 cells and 350–500 kW/m
2
for 26650 cells. These findings quantify the dynamic flammability of off-gases and highlight the importance of spatially resolved measurements for fire hazard assessment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion battery
Heat flux measurements
Thermal runaway
Thermal hazard
Lithium iron phosphate

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation of samples.
Experimental set-up. For a schematic illustration of the cell preparation and the experimental set-up (with dimensions), respectively, see Figs. 1 and 3.
Thermocouple array over the cell.
Heat flux gauges arranged above and adjacent to the cell.
The cell, wrapped in glass cloth tape, mounted on the load cell.
Experimental set-up with heat flux gauges and thermocouples.

Cell surface temperature (
T
cell
) and gas temperature above cell (
T
1
−
T
2
) from 18650 cell experiment with forced ignition of the vent gases. In plots the time of venting (
t
vent
) and the time of onset of thermal runaway (
t
TR
) are marked with dashed lines..

Heat flux measurements from 18650 cell experiment with forced ignition of the vent gases. Left: horizontal gauges. Right: vertical gauges. In plots the time of venting (
t
vent
) and the time of onset of thermal runaway (
t
TR
) are marked with dashed lines.

Peak radiative heat flux during the two flaming stages (Flame 1 and Flame 2) measured at different radial (left) and vertical (right) distances from the cell. Error bars indicate measurement uncertainty.
Stages of the forced ignition experiment for the 18650 cells.
Key events from stages 1, 2 and 3 of the 18650 cell forced ignition experiment. Steps A to K are defined in Table 4, and the corresponding time stamps are noted for this exemplar experiment.
Cell surface and gas temperature recorded during 18650 cell experiments without forced ignition.
Left: Cell surface and gas temperature recorded during 26650 cell experiments with forced ignition. Right: Heat flux measurements.
Key events from stages 1 and 2 of the 26650 cell forced ignition experiment. Steps A to H are defined in Table 5, and the corresponding time stamps are noted for this exemplar experiment.
Heat flux measurements from HF1 and HF3 during a 18650 cell forced ignition experiment with the two distinct peaks visible. Position of heat flux gauges HF1 and HF3 shown in sketch on the right.

Peak heat flux for 18650 and 26650 experiment with forced ignition measured by heat flux gauges HF1 and HF3 for the first flaming event after gas venting (peak 1) and second flaming event after cell enters thermal runaway (peak 2).
Total radiative heat flux (time-integrated) measured by HF1 and HF3 for 18650 and 26650 LFP cells during the first and second flaming stages.

Cell surface temperature as venting,
T
vent
, and maximum cell surface temperature,
T
max
, during 18650 and 26650 cell experiments with forced ignition.
T
vent
and
T
max
defined in sketch in the right.

Total mass loss for 18650 with and without forced ignition of the vent gases and the 26650 cell experiments with forced ignition. Left: Total mass loss as a percentage of the initial mass, Right: Total mass loss in grams.
Comparison between mass loss for LFP 18650 cells at 100% SOC undergoing thermal runaway reported in the literature [34–37] and the mean experimental values from this study, shown with 1 σ error bars.
Temperature profile of the heated 18650 cell and gas temperature vertically above the cell. Vertical dotted lines indicate onset of thermal runaway.
Heat flux from 18650 cell. The three main stages are shaded.
Temperature of the heated cell and gas temperature vertically above the cell from the 26650 experiments with external ignition.
Heat flux from 26650 cell experiments with external ignition.
Specifications of the cells used in the current study.
Charge cycle parameters.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: X = Initial voltage state, inherently variable.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Summary of the experiments.

Description of the evolution of gas and particulate emissions and flame characteristics for the 18650 cells undergoing thermal runaway with a spark plug ignition source active. The key events summarised here are shown in Fig. 8.

row.entry: Initially, gas vents at high velocity, flames are tilted and have a directionality due to vent shape and location
The gas production slows down and flames become more buoyant, vertical and laminar
Low initial rate of gas emission from the cell
Gradual increase in the quantity of white off-gas without ignition. A small 1–2 cm flame appears attached to the cell vent but cannot self-sustain
Gas production increases further with a thick white cloud gathering above the cell
A hissing noise can be heard as gas production increases and gases are released under pressure. Fractions of seconds after, the vented gases re-ignite
Hot ejected particles are seen flying to the ground. Flame is quite violent and turbulent, seeming to have engulfed the whole cell
Flame becomes more buoyant and laminar

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Description of the evolution of gas and particulate emissions and flame characteristics for the 26650 cells undergoing thermal runaway with a spark plug ignition source active. The key events summarised here are shown in Fig. 11.

row.entry: Initially, gas vents at high velocity, flames are tilted and have a directionality due to vent shape and location
The gas production slows down and flames become more buoyant and laminar
The flame shrinks but does not fully extinguish.
A hissing noise can be heard as gas production increases. The gases exit from the vents at a very high velocity, and the flames at times cannot be sustained (strain rate too high).
A highly turbulent flame develops and hot ejected particles are seen flying to the ground.
Flame is quite violent and turbulent.
Flame becomes more buoyant and laminar until its abatement

ce:caption.ce:simple-para:
Estimated Biot numbers for 18650 and 26650 Li-ion cells, calculated for radial thermal conductivities (
k
r
), with the mean, standard deviation and 95th percentile reported at various cell surface temperatures.
Comparison of temperature at venting and total mass loss for LFP 18650 cells undergoing thermal runaway found in the literature.

row.entry: Temperature at start of cell venting [°C]

head.ce:title: Experimental study on the radiative heat flux from flaming 18650- and 26650-type LFP battery cells

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Fires from lithium-ion cells are a major heat transfer pathway, promoting thermal runaway propagation and spreading flames to surrounding cells. This study presents new experimental insights into the fire behaviour of lithium iron phosphate (LFP) cells by quantifying radiative heat flux, gas temperature, and cell surface temperature during venting and flaming. The key contributions are: (i) the first spatially and time-resolved measurements of radiative heat flux from single-cell LFP fires, revealing non-uniform radiation with peak intensities varying up to
∼ 10 ×
across the horizontal plane; and (ii) a controlled comparison of 18650 and 26650 cylindrical cells, demonstrating fundamental differences in combustion behaviour and peak radiative output.

Fully charged cells were heated under constant power to induce thermal runaway, with and without an external ignition source. Three gas emission stages were identified: (1) steady emission post-venting, (2) increased gas production with self-heating, and (3) rapid gas release at thermal runaway. Forced ignition sustained flames in stages 1 and 3 for 18650 cells, whereas 26650 cells maintained combustion throughout. Peak radiative heat fluxes reached ∼ 150 kW/m
2
for 18650 cells and 350–500 kW/m
2
for 26650 cells.
These findings quantify the dynamic flammability of off-gases and highlight the importance of spatially resolved measurements for fire hazard assessment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para:
Research Link Provided
University of Sheffield Data Repository Experimental Temperature and Heat Flux Data for Constant Power Over Heating Tests of 18650 and 26650 LFP Cells. (Original data)
.

ce:section.ce:para: Batteries are a cornerstone of modern technology, powering a wide variety of applications, from portable electronics to electric vehicles (EVs) and large-scale Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS). Among various technologies, lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) have established a dominant position in the market thanks to their high energy density, long service life, and versatility. Within this domain, LIBs with lithium iron phosphate (LFP) cathodes have emerged as a leading choice due to their high rate capabilities and relatively low cost. These attributes have been ensuring that LFP battery cells stay at the forefront of applications in BESS and EVs. Forecasts expect the market share of LFP for BESS to rise from approximately 60% in 2022 to over 70% by 2030 [1], making them a pivotal technology in the global transition towards sustainable energy solutions.

To understand the fire risk associated with lithium-ion batteries, in the last 20 years, the scientific community has invested significant resources to test batteries of different chemistries and formats [2,3], as well as developing models that try to describe the complex multiphysics that occurs during the thermal runaway process [4–6].

Focusing solely on experimental work with LFP cells to narrow the scope of this discussion, both cylindrical cells (e.g. Refs. [7,8]) and prismatic cells (e.g. [9–12]) have been studied and reported to exhibit a low probability of flame self-ignition during thermal runaway events, requiring an external ignition source in single-cell laboratory experiments. This behaviour has incorrectly enhanced their perceived safety; however, other safety risks need to be considered. For example, Zhang et al. [13] analysed the gaseous products from LFP and NMC cathodes and concluded that LFP batteries pose a higher risk of explosion and emit more toxic products that contribute to environmental pollution and harm human health. Whilst experiments on large-scale electrical storage systems showed that failure of one module leads to self-ignition and flame propagation across the whole system without the need of an external ignition source [14].

Parameters frequently measured to quantify the fire risk are the cell surface temperature, the heat release rate, and the mass loss. Although LFP cells require more heat to initiate thermal runaway compared to other chemistries [7,15], in a review on gas emissions these cells have also been found to produce more hydrogen as a fraction of total composition than NMC cells [16], a highly flammable gas. Furthermore, total normalised heat release from flames with respect to nominal energy was found to be highest for LFP cells [3]. However, it is important to note that the variation in measurements compared across different experimental methods was substantial; therefore, definitive conclusions are to some degree speculative.

Cell surface temperature and gas temperature, such as that of flames or smoke, are commonly used metrics for comparing the thermal runaway behaviour of cells [12,17,18]. However, these temperature measurements are highly sensitive to the characteristics of the measuring device. For instance, the thickness of thermocouples can significantly influence the recorded values; thicker thermocouples often introduce thermal lag when capturing gas temperatures and may produce noisy data [19]. Furthermore, temperature is inherently dependent on the properties of the medium being measured, such as its mass and density, as it reflects the thermal state of the medium. Smoke temperature, in particular, is influenced by smoke dynamics and may vary considerably with movement, making it an unreliable metric unless a uniform smoke layer is present. Therefore, extracting temperature data without context is not recommended. This context can be provided by measuring the combustion heat release rate (HRR) and flame heat flux, which allows quantification of battery flame heat output and hazard potential.

The heat released from flames was found to be three times the internal heat generated by the thermal decomposition of cell materials [20] undergoing thermal runaway, and therefore flames play a significant role in thermal runaway propagation. As such, heat flux is a more accurate and useful quantity than temperature alone to study and calculate the effects and consequences of battery fires. Heat flux gauges, by isolating the modes of heat transfer, allow us to measure and quantify radiative heat flux, which is a major heat transfer mode from the flames [21]. Further, through the use of heat flux gauges, the convective heat release can be calculated from the total chemical heat release, determined by HRR (oxygen consumption calorimetry) measurements, minus the radiative heat.

Previous experiments measured radiative heat flux for NMC and LFP [22] and NCA [23] 18650 cylindrical cells, as well as for a module with four NMC 18650 cells [24], and a module of four LCO 18650 cells [25]. However, in all experiments, the heat flux was measured at a single point adjacent to the battery vent, not allowing for any conclusions on the spatial resolution of the heat flux from the flames. More recently, Mao et al. 2024 [11] measured heat flux from a prismatic LFP 280 Ah cell, and this is, to our best knowledge, the first study exploring the spatial resolution with five heat flux gauges positioned vertically along the flame height. Spatially resolved heat flux measurements, radially from the flame and vertically parallel to the flame, are important for quantifying the non-uniformity of heat exposure and therefore the implications on surrounding structures, as well as for the validation of fire models.

This study presents new experimental insights into the fire behaviour of lithium ion cells by focusing on radiative heat flux emission, gas temperature, and cell surface temperature during venting and flaming. The key novelties of the work are:

(i) the combined, time- and spatially-resolved measurement of radiative heat flux, together with gas temperature and cell surface temperature, enabling a direct characterisation of flame intensity and thermal exposure from single cell fires; and

(ii) a controlled experimental comparison between two LFP cylindrical cell formats, 18650 and 26650, allowing the influence of cell form factor on radiative output and thermal behaviour to be isolated.

The experiments were conducted on individual LFP cells of both sizes, tested with and without an external ignition source as discussed in Section 2. Distinct stages of venting and flaming behaviour are identified for each format in Section 3. In Section 4, differences in peak radiative heat flux, gas temperature, and cell surface temperature are quantified and compared, revealing systematic variations in flame intensity and thermal hazard between the 18650 and 26650 cells. Previous work has focused on single point measurements of heat flux from flaming cells. This does not allow conclusions on location of maximum heat flux from flames and does not reveal much about the flame height and shape. The more granular data presented here allows for the first time to identify the radiation field from a flaming LFP cell. These results provide new data for fire hazard assessment and for the development and validation of physics-based models of lithium-ion battery fires.

Commercial cylindrical LFP cells of two sizes, 18650 and 26650, were chosen for comparison. All cells used in the experiments were approximately two years old from the date of manufacture and procured from a reputable UK electrical supplier. Once delivered, the cells were stored at room temperature and in a dry environment. The properties of each pristine cell are provided in Table 1.

To ensure experimental consistency, all cells were fully charged using a BST8-30A-CDS Battery Analyser prior to testing. During the cycling process, a thermocouple was attached to each cell, serving two functions: temperature monitoring and safety control. The thermocouple enabled software-driven interruption of the cycling process if the cell temperature exceeded a predetermined threshold.

A standardised charge cycle was applied to all cells, which began with a full discharge at 0.5 C-rate to 2.8 V, followed by a charging sequence of 0.5 C-rate constant current (CC) to a maximum voltage of 3.7 V and then a constant voltage (CV) until a cut of current of 0.05 C-rate, as detailed in Table 2. The initial discharge was necessary to address the unknown initial state-of-charge (SOC) of the cells, thereby eliminating potential variability. Throughout the entire dis/charge cycling, temperature, time, voltage, current and capacity were recorded.

Prior to the experiments, the plastic coating was then removed, and the cell was weighed to record an initial mass. A thermocouple was secured to the cell using a thermally and electrically resistant glass cloth tape. To prevent short-circuiting of the nickel-chromium wire heating element, the cell was then wrapped in a layer of glass-cloth tape. Following this, a 35 cm long heating wire was wrapped around the tape-covered cell and secured in place using Kapton tape. After instrumentation, the cell was weighed again to determine the additional mass. Fig. 1 illustrates the steps taken to prepare each cell for the experiments.

The experiments were performed inside a large steel experimental chamber (2 × 2 × 6 m), shown in Fig. 2, with Figs. 2(a) to 2(b) and 2(c) showing the equipment set up within. The chamber was left open to the environment on one side to provide ventilation, preventing gas accumulation and reducing the risk of overpressure or explosion. As such, a light draft could flow through the experiment chamber, depending on wind direction and magnitude.

The cells were placed vertically (vent facing upwards) in a sample holder secured to a load cell (Fig. 2(c)). The two ends of the heating element had crocodile clips applied to connect the heater power supply. The surface-bonded thermocouple was connected to a Pico TC08 thermocouple data logger.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Heat flux, temperature, and mass measurements
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 3 shows the set-up with heat flux gauges and thermocouples. The radiative heat flux from the flames was measured using six Hukseflux SBG01 heat flux sensors located vertically and horizontally from the battery vent. The sensors were rated at 50 kW/m2 and the calibration uncertainty reported is of
± 2 %
The closest heat flux sensor was located 150 mm from the cell (in a radial direction), with the subsequent horizontal sensor spaced 50 mm further away. The lowest vertical sensor is located 50 mm horizontally from the second horizontal sensor. The four vertically arranged heat flux gauges were positioned at 250 mm intervals, the same vertical distance from the safety valve as the thermocouples. The gauges are designed to measure thermal radiation while minimising the measured convection contribution.

K-type thermocouples with 1.5 mm diameter were used to measure the gas temperature. The lowest thermocouple is positioned 20 mm above the cell vents, along the cell’s centre line, to capture the temperature of the flame base. These thermocouples were vertically arranged at 250 mm intervals. The intrinsic error of K-type thermocouples is taken as
± 2
°C.

A spark igniter was placed just above the cell vent slightly to the side (external) to avoid interfering with gas flow dynamics. As the cells tested had LFP cathodes, the maximum temperature of the cells during thermal runaway would not reach the self-ignition temperature for the off-gases. Hence, the igniter was used to generate flames.

The heating element had a constant power run through it, see Table 3. The applied heating power was normalised against cell capacity to take account of the larger thermal mass of the 26650 cell compared to the 18650 cells. The constant heating would be applied until five minutes post-TR. If the experiment included a forced ignition, the igniter was activated when the heating element was switched on and would remain on throughout the experiment.

Alongside the surface-bonded thermocouple, the heat flux sensors, and flame thermocouples were connected to Pico TC-08 thermocouple DAQs. The heater current was recorded by a multimeter DAQ capabilities, while the heat voltage was recorded by PicoLog 1012 Voltage Data Logger.

To minimise errors and improve repeatability between tests, the positions of the heat flux gauges and thermocouples were checked before each test. In addition, the heat flux sensors were cleaned with an alcohol solution. This limited the build-up of soot and dirt, which can significantly influence measurements by blocking the radiation reaching the sensors.

After the thermal runaway event had finished and the cell had cooled, the thermocouple and heating elements were disconnected from the DAQs/power supply, but were left attached to the cell. The cell was then weighed and assessed to characterise any damage (such as tearing/buckling of the cell casing etc.). The cell mass loss was calculated as the difference in mass before and after the experiment of the combined cell and instrumentation (thermocouple, tape, and heater wire).

The thermocouple and heat flux data for one representative 18650 cell experiment with forced ignition of the vent gases are shown in Figs. 4 and 5 respectively. Two main events are marked in all plots, the gas venting (
t
vent
) and the onset of thermal runaway (
t
TR
). The venting time was verified against the video recording and is shown in the plots. The onset of thermal runaway was defined as the time when the rate of change of the cell temperature,
t
cell
, with respect to time,
d T / d t
, was greater than 2.5 °C/s (i.e. the gradient of the curve) which was found to be the best value from trial and error. Similar values have been chosen in previous work.

The cell surface temperature rapidly increases at the time the heater is switched on, while the temperature rate reduces as the cell tends towards thermal equilibrium. At approximately 120 s it can be seen the cell temperature begins to rise, away from thermal equilibrium, as self-heating due to material decomposition begins. Further, after venting, it can be seen that the cell temperature rate noticeably increases. Once the cell reaches thermal runaway, the cell temperature increases exponentially as the exothermic reactions inside the battery progress. A few seconds after the onset of thermal runaway, the cell surface reaches its maximum temperature of approx. 600 °C and then cools relatively slowly (note, as mentioned in the methodology, the heater remains on).

Of the thermocouples placed above the cell vents, only
T
1
measured high temperatures, as this was the only thermocouple engulfed in hot gases. The first peak of
T
1
occurs a few seconds after venting, when the build-up of gases inside the cell is released to the environment as a result of the opening of the safety vents and are ignited by the spark. Although the gases continue to be produced even after venting,
T
1
cools down (100 °C) as the flames slowly abate and are extinguished (note, the spark igniter remains on).

The gauges pick up some radiation for a few seconds after venting. Subsequently, as the flames abate, the measurements drop until the flame is extinguished and the gauge stops picking up any radiation. When the cell finally goes into thermal runaway and the gases reignite, the heat flux shows another peak, which in most cases is greater than the first peak.

As discussed in the next section, there is a stage in between the first flaming event and the second flaming event, when decomposition gases continue being released to the atmosphere but do not ignite.

The spatial non-uniformity of the radiation field is clearly visible in Fig. 6. The figure shows the variation of peak radiative heat flux with radial and vertical distance from the cell during the two flaming stages. The thermocouples positioned on the horizontal plane, H1 was located 150 mm from the edge of the vent and H2 at 200 mm. Whilst the thermocouple on the vertical plane were located at 250 mm from the edge of the vent and varying heights above the vent. Overall, the data demonstrate a strong spatial dependence of radiation intensity, confirming that the thermal exposure from single-cell fires is highly non-uniform.

In the radial direction, the peak heat flux decreases with increasing distance from the cell for both flaming stages, consistent with the reduction in radiative view factor and increased attenuation with distance. Flame 2 generally exhibits higher peak values than Flame 1 at equivalent locations, indicating a stronger radiative output immediately after thermal runaway.

In the vertical direction, the heat flux profiles reveal pronounced asymmetry along the flame height. Flame 2 shows higher peak values close to the vent region, followed by a rapid decrease at intermediate heights, indicating that the most intense radiative emission is concentrated near the flame base where combustion is strongest. Conversely, some locations at higher elevation exhibit increased heat flux during Flame 1, reflecting flame elongation and buoyant plume development. These results highlight that vertical position strongly influences local thermal loading and that single-point measurements would not capture the observed directional variability. The spatial gradients demonstrated here emphasise the importance of using spatially resolved measurements when assessing radiative heating and potential flame-induced propagation to neighbouring cells.

During the 18650 experiments with forced ignition of vented gases, three distinct stages have been identified as shown in Fig. 7. The first stage initiates with the opening of the safety valve due to the pressure build-up of decomposition gases inside the cell casing. The gases are ignited by the spark igniter as soon as they are released. This stage terminated once the flames self-extinguished and had an average duration of 1 min 40 s across the 3 experiments. We will refer to this stage as Flame 1. The main events are summarised in Table 4 and captured in Fig. 8.

The second stage starts once Flame 1 is extinguished. This stage does not involve sustained or substantial flaming (nuanced behaviour is presented in the supplementary material). The igniter remained continuously activated; however, sustained combustion was not observed. The vent gases therefore did not ignite under these conditions, which is likely associated with changes in gas composition and/or local mixing conditions rather than the absence of an ignition source.

During this stage, the gas temperature recorded by
T
1
cools down rapidly due to the lack of burning and stabilises at ca. 100 °C, which is visible in Fig. 7. Simultaneously, the rate of increase of the cell surface temperature rose again, suggesting an increase in the decomposition rate within the cell. The average duration of this second stage is 1 min 20 s.

Based on observations reported in previous studies, the initially emitted gas at venting is expected to contain a higher fraction of solvent vapour, which is readily ignitable, whereas later stages of venting have been reported to contain larger fractions of non-flammable species such as CO2 until thermal runaway, when additional hydrocarbons may be produced [26,27]. Although gas composition was not measured directly in the present work, the observed transition from sustained flaming to a non-flaming stage is consistent with such trends reported in the literature. Consequently, the absence of flames during this period is interpreted as likely associated with increased dilution of the fuel mixture and reduced overall flammability.

Finally, the last stage identified and consistent across all forced ignition experiments (including the larger 26650 experiments) is here referred to as the second flaming stage. This stage initiates with the onset of thermal runaway, as the cell surface temperature records an exponential growth and the rate of thermal decomposition reaction increases. The gas composition, air-fuel mixing condition or flow dynamics change at this point, and the vent gases ignite, producing a much larger and more turbulent flame. This stage differs from the first flaming stage not only in terms of flame speed but also in the emergence of hot burning particles ejected from the cell, landing up to 35 cm from the cell vent. A detailed summary of the process is given in Table 4.

For the 18650 cells heated but without an external ignition source applied, no flaming was observed. The main conclusion is that these cell formats, when undergoing thermal runaway due to external heating, will produce large quantities of decomposition gases that will only ignite if an external ignition source is present. These findings agree with previous research [11]. However, these conclusions are only valid for a single cell undergoing thermal runaway in a similar environment to our test environment (atmospheric pressure, ambient temperature ca. 10 °C). What is not clear is whether the gases would ignite if multiple cells were undergoing thermal runaway at the same time or if an increased ambient pressure or temperature would facilitate self-ignition of the vented gases.

The thermocouple data is presented in Fig. 9. The cell surface temperature follows the same trend as the experiments with external ignition and flaming; however, the temperature experienced by the cell is lower, and the cell takes longer to undergo thermal runaway. This is because of the absence of heat from the combustion of the vented gases, which, when present, will provide additional heat to the cell by radiation emitted downwards and accelerate the decomposition reaction. Therefore, the cell temperature peaks at 200 °C lower than the experiment with external ignition, and the onset of thermal runaway is delayed by approximately 1 min.

Fig. 10 presents the temperature and radiative heat flux recorded during a 26650 experiment. Additional plots for all 26650 cell forced ignition experiments can be found in Appendix B. The cell surface temperature follows a similar trend to that observed for the 18650 cells: an initial steep increase caused by the activation of the power supply, followed by a slower quasi-linear rise as heat is conducted from the cell surface to its internal layers. Subsequently, a sudden temperature spike occurs at the onset of thermal runaway, driven by exothermic decomposition reactions inside the cell. After reaching a peak value of approximately 600 °C, the surface temperature decreases as these internal reactions cease and heat dissipates to the environment.

The gas temperature, also shown in Fig. 10, reveals that the thermocouple nearest the vent (
T
1
) reaches a maximum between 600 °C and 700 °C, consistent with the values observed in the 18650 experiments. In contrast, the upper thermocouple (
T
2
) records a peak temperature between 50 and 100 °C, slightly higher compared to peak values recorded during the smaller cell experiment.

Heat flux measurements display two distinct peaks: the first occurring immediately after venting, and the second coinciding with the onset of thermal runaway. The peak heat flux during this second event is substantially higher than that recorded in the smaller cell experiments.

Table 5 and Fig. 11 summarise and illustrate the key stages observed during the 26650 cell experiments. Notably, flames from the larger cells exhibited a slightly different behaviour compared to those of the smaller cells: gases ignited upon venting and persisted until the conclusion of thermal runaway, extinguishing only once gas generation ceased. Consequently, as shown in Fig. 10, the temperature recorded by
T
1
does not exhibit the sharp drop observed in the 18650 experiments, where flame extinguishment resulted in cooling of the surroundings.

The generation of particle ejecta during thermal runaway events is closely linked to the cell type and its specific failure characteristics. This section examines the behaviour of particle ejecta under two distinct scenarios: one involving external ignition and flaming, and the other without any external ignition.

In the flaming case, corresponding to Fig. 4, the 18650-type cell exhibited a peak temperature of approximately 550 °C. This elevated temperature is likely due to the additional heat emitted by the external flame, which can further accelerate decomposition reactions within the cell. As shown by Li et al. [28], temperature plays a critical role in the generation of particle ejecta. At higher temperatures, secondary reactions are triggered within the cell materials, resulting in increased particle generations. Fig. 8 supports this, showing a visible trace of particle ejecta dispersing from the cell into the surrounding environment during flaming combustion. Additionally, elevated temperatures can lead to higher internal pressure [28], which contributes to the more forceful ejection of materials. Overall, the presence of flame introduced an additional energy source that significantly intensified the cell’s failure dynamics.

In contrast, the non-flaming case, as shown in Fig. 9, demonstrated a less severe failure process. In this case, the maximum temperature reached around 470 °C. Without external ignition, thermal runaway progressed primarily through internal decomposition and gas venting. The absence of flame meant that the released gases did not ignite, and no secondary thermal input was introduced. As a result, both temperature rise and pressure build-up occurred more gradually. Consequently, there was a markedly lower degree of particle ejection, and no visible trace of ejecta dispersing into the environment.

None of the cells showed outward signs of deformation or damage, other than the presence of black matter in the ignition case and solidification of a brown substance under the non-ignition case. This solidified, brownish substance was witnessed to pool on the cell cap, drip down the cell side and build up on the thermocouple directly above the cell vent. Further, work is needed to analyse this material, but it is hypothesised that it is a mixture of electrolyte and solid mass residue that condenses out of the off-gas/vapour cloud when not ignited. This is supported by the observation of electrolytes turning brown under degradation and decomposition [29].

These observations highlight the significant influence of external ignition on the severity of thermal runaway in lithium-ion cells. The ignition of vented gases not only introduced additional heat but also amplified internal pressure, both of which contributed to increased particle ejecta. These findings have important implications for battery safety analysis, particularly in scenarios where external ignition sources are present or in systems susceptible to flaming propagation between adjacent cells.

Understanding and quantifying the dynamics of a single-cell fire and its heat output is a necessary first step before addressing fires at the module or pack level. Our experimental set-up intentionally simplifies the problem by reducing the number of interacting variables and unknowns.

The experiments were conducted in an open environment with natural ventilation in order to avoid gas accumulation and overpressure and to allow safe, repeatable testing of single-cell failures. This configuration inevitably influences flame behaviour, heat transfer, and gas-phase combustion compared to confined or semi-confined real-world scenarios such as battery modules, packs, or energy storage enclosures. Therefore the reported radiative heat flux values are most representative of isolated, single-cell fires without enclosure effects. The measured values should primarily be interpreted for relative comparison between cell formats and combustion stages rather than as absolute design values for enclosed battery systems.

In contrast to confined environments, heat transfer to the surroundings is enhanced, and the cell is cooled more rapidly by ambient air. As a consequence, the cell temperatures measured in our tests are lower than would be expected for a cell undergoing thermal runaway inside a module or pack, where much of the generated heat would be retained.

The present dataset isolates the intrinsic combustion behaviour of individual cells, whereas enclosure effects represent a separate scaling problem governed by ventilation-controlled combustion and radiative feedback.

To assess the variability of each cell type and compare between the two cell formats, box plots of the peak heat flux, total radiative heat flux, cell surface temperature, and total mass loss are presented here. For all box plots in this paper, the shaded box is the interquartile range, the horizontal line within the shaded box is the median, and the whiskers are the minimum and maximum values. Individual data points are displayed as red scatter points.

The heat flux measured during experiments without forced ignition was close to zero, as no flaming was present. Therefore, only experiments with forced ignition are included in the peak heat flux analysis, while the surface temperature and mass loss plots include data from both experiments, with and without forced ignition.

Ageing may influence thermal runaway behaviour through changes in internal heat generation, gas generation rate, or vent opening pressure, which could in turn affect venting behaviour, flame duration, and peak radiative output. However, the cells within each format were sourced from the same batch and tested under identical experimental conditions, which helps reduce variability associated with ageing within each group. Consequently, while ageing-related effects may contribute to scatter between individual tests, they are unlikely to systematically alter the main observed trends between the two cell formats.

Peak heat flux values are compared for two of the six heat flux gauges. The selected gauges were HF1 and HF3, shown in Fig. 12, which are, respectively, the closest horizontal and vertical gauges to the cell vents. Consequently, the highest heat fluxes were recorded by these two gauges.

In the open-air configuration used here, variations in flame position due to ambient drafts contribute to scatter in the peak heat flux measurements, as the effective view factor between the flame and a given gauge changes over time. The relative differences observed between cell formats (18650 versus 26650) are expected to remain valid, as they are driven primarily by differences in gas generation rate, flame size, and energy release rather than by ventilation alone.

The peak heat flux of the first flaming event was approximately 50–150 kW/m2 for most cells but reached 280 kW/m2 for one of the 26650 cell, as shown in Fig. 13. The initial flame was attributed to the combustion of the solvent electrolyte vapours. For 18650 cells, the second peak was found to be approximately 150 kW/m2, whereas 26650 cells reached 350–500 kW/m2. This second flaming stage was more violent, leading to larger flames as well as visible burning ejected particles.

The scatter of data points is substantial, especially for the larger-sized cells. The measured heat flux is largely dependent on the view factor and the distance of the flames from the measuring device. The closer the flame to the gauge, the larger the amount of flame visible by the device, and consequently, the larger the radiative flux received. As seen in Section 3, the flames are very transient and will move in position depending on the location of the vent and the presence of drafts. However, even within this uncertainty, the peak heat flux measured from the 26650 cells is statistically greater than the peak heat flux from the 18650 cells, for both flaming events and for both gauges, as shown by the median values in Fig. 13. The findings suggest that cell size and capacity should be considered when evaluating fire hazards. Therefore, selecting small-sized batteries is crucial when safety concerns dominate in a specific application.

In addition to peak radiative heat flux, the time-integrated radiative output was evaluated to quantify the total radiative heat flux delivered during each flaming stage. Fig. 14 presents box plots of the total radiative heat flux measured by HF1 and HF3 for both cell formats and for the first and second flaming events. The total heat flux was calculated by integrating the measured heat flux over time for each flaming stage, thereby capturing differences in both flame intensity and flame duration.

For both gauges, the 26650 cells exhibited substantially higher total radiative heat flux than the 18650 cells. At HF1, the median total radiative heat flux from 26650 cells was approximately 2–3 times larger than that of the 18650 cells during the first flaming event, and increased further during the second flaming event, reflecting the more energetic combustion following the onset of thermal runaway. A similar trend is observed at HF3, where the second flaming stage dominates the radiative output for both formats.

The comparison between HF1 and HF3 also highlights the influence of spatial non-uniformity. For each cell type, the total heat flux recorded by HF3 is greater than that measured by HF1, consistent with the closer alignment of HF3 with the main flame region during the second flaming event. This confirms that local radiation exposure can vary by several times depending on position relative to the vent and dominant flame zone.

Importantly, the second flaming stage accounts for the majority of the total radiative heat flux for both cell sizes. This indicates that, although the first flaming event upon venting produces measurable radiation, the dominant contribution to heat dose arises after the onset of thermal runaway, when gas release rates and flame turbulence increase markedly. These findings reinforce that cell format and capacity strongly influence both instantaneous peak radiation and cumulative radiative heat dose, with clear implications for thermal runaway propagation in closely packed modules.

Cell surface temperature is an important variable in thermal runaway experiments; it gives an indication of the cell’s internal temperature. Further, as the heat applied on the surface will quickly transfer to the centre of the cell, as 18650’s have a low Biot number for most of the cases studied here as expanded on in Section 4.4 in detail, the surface temperature is appropriate for estimating the internal temperature [30]. This heat will initiate the degradation of the active components of the battery (SEI layer, electrodes, electrolyte, and separator), and as these materials react, they will produce more heat. The initial slower cell heating phase will be dominated by the heat provided by the heater (external heating), whilst the sudden increase in cell temperature indicates the onset of thermal runaway. During this second phase, internal heating from the chemical reactions will dominate. When the reactions are completed and no more internal heat is produced, the cell starts to cool down.

Here we analyse two cell surface temperature values, the temperature at the time of venting,
T
vent
, and the maximum temperature experienced,
T
max
. The temperature at venting can be useful as it indicates when the decomposition gases are released to the environment and consequently when the first flames can appear. This is essential information when studying or modelling thermal runaway propagation, as the flames will release more heat than that which is transferred from the cell’s surface, while the gas flow can transport heat to cells other than the direct neighbours. The maximum temperature, on the other hand, can indicate the intensity of the thermal runaway process and is a useful value to compare between cell formats.

Fig. 15 presents the two values side by side, as well as a diagram of how
T
vent
and
T
max
are defined. For both cell formats, the temperatures are comparable; however, the 26650 exhibits a slightly higher peak temperature. This increase may be attributed to the longer flaming combustion stage observed in the 26650 experiments, which supplies additional heat to the cell and accelerates thermal decomposition reactions.

When no external ignition is applied, the vented gases do not ignite, and no flames are present during these experiments. Thus, the peak cell surface temperature is lower than for experiments with forced ignition, with an average
T
max
of 580 °C and 460 °C, for experiments with flames and without, respectively. This has implications in risk assessments, as the occurrence of flames leading to higher cell temperatures results in greater material decomposition and, therefore, greater production of gases. The temperature of venting for experiments with flames and without,
T
vent
of 230 °C and 190 °C respectively, shows similar behaviour. However, as flaming can only occur after venting, the difference is attributed to inherent variation in the samples, and further runs are required to determine a higher certainty on the median and range of the vent temperature.

To assess whether internal temperature gradients within the cell are significant during TR, venting, and flaming, the Biot number ( Bi ) was evaluated for each Li-ion cell type throughout the entire duration of each experiment. As the measurement of the anisotropic thermal conductivity of the cells is not readily accessible, literature values are employed to estimate Bi . As such, to quantify the uncertainty in Bi , a Monte Carlo simulation (
N = 1
0
4
iterations) was performed to propagate the combined variances of experimental measurement and material properties.

The analysis utilised a non-parametric bootstrapping approach with replacement to sample from the discrete experimental datasets (cell surface and ambient environment temperatures), thereby preserving the inherent measurement correlations without assuming a specific error distribution for the temperature inputs. Simultaneously, the cell’s thermal conductivity was sampled from a probability distribution to account for material variability. Given the anisotropic nature of thermal conductivity, where the radial (
k
r
) and axial (
k
z
) values are summarised as
k
r
= 0 . 83
W/m/K
, IQR
0 . 42 − 2 . 94
W/m/K
;
k
z
= 13 . 6
W/m/K
, IQR
8 . 41 − 22 . 5
W/m/K
[31], only the limiting radial condition, with low conductivity and relatively large surface area for heat loss, is considered. The probability distribution for
k
r
was represented using a non-parametric kernel density estimate fitted to literature values reported in Ref. [31]. An Epanechnikov kernel was employed with a fixed bandwidth of
1
W/m/K
, providing a smooth probability density function without assuming an underlying parametric form.

For each iteration, the natural convection coefficient was determined using the Churchill–Chu correlation [32] modified by Popiel’s correction factor ( ξ ) [33], which accounts for curvature enhancement in narrow cylinders. Under the condition where
D ≥ 35 L / G
r
L
0 . 25
is not satisfied, as in the case here, the average Nusselt number for the vertical cylinder is calculated as
Nu
H , c y
=
Nu
H , f p
( 1 + ξ )
, where
Nu
H , f p
is the average Nusselt number for a vertical flat plate.

The total mass loss after thermal runaway for all 18650 cell experiments with and without external ignition and for 22650 cell experiments with external ignition can be seen in Fig. 16. For the 18650 cells, the mass loss without external ignition is consistently lower than for experiments with external ignition. This can be linked to the presence of flames that will provide additional heat through radiation emitted downwards, which will exacerbate the thermal decomposition of the battery materials and lead to more gas generation and ejected particles, and thus mass loss.

The larger size cells exhibit greater mass loss in absolute terms as their initial mass is higher, and consequently, there is more active material available for the reactions. However, when comparing the percentage of mass loss, for both cell formats, the average mass loss is approximately 17.3%.

A range of reported [17,34–37] values for the temperature at which LFP 18650 cells began venting and the final mass loss from other works is presented in Table 7. Venting temperatures do not appear to follow any particular pattern, likely a result of design choices for the pressure limit of the vents. In general, any comparison with existing literature is beset by the lack of disclosure with regards the physical characteristics of the cells used, be it the vent properties, the construction of the casing or the finer details of electrode composition, either in the publications themselves, or in the specification documents provided by manufacturers. However, there is a clear negative correlation between capacity and relative mass loss, as shown in Fig. 17. The mass loss found by this work is slightly below expectations for the capacity of the cell used, but is within the range seen in the literature for LFP cells.

Compared with large-format prismatic LFP cells reported in the literature, the cylindrical cells investigated in this study exhibit distinct fire and venting characteristics. Prismatic cells typically release gases through larger vent areas, often producing high-momentum jet-like flows and more continuous gas discharge, whereas cylindrical cells show stronger directionality governed by localised safety vents. This results in more pronounced flame asymmetry and localised radiative exposure for cylindrical formats. In prismatic cells, combustion is often characterised by sustained jet flames and longer combustion duration associated with larger gas inventories and internal free volume [11], while the cylindrical cells investigated here demonstrate shorter flaming stages with stronger temporal variability and intermittent combustion behaviour.

These differences are primarily attributed to structural and design factors. Cylindrical cells possess a smaller internal volume and concentrated venting location, which promote directional jetting and strong spatial gradients in heat flux. In contrast, the larger vent areas and structural geometry of prismatic cells facilitate higher gas release rates, greater combustion stability, and more spatially distributed flame structures. Consequently, radiative heat flux distributions observed for cylindrical cells show greater local non-uniformity, whereas prismatic cell fires tend to produce larger but more spatially extended flame regions. These distinctions highlight the importance of considering cell geometry and vent design when extrapolating fire behaviour across battery formats.

Fires from Li-ion cells present a major heat transfer pathway, promoting thermal runaway propagation in Li-ion batteries and flames spread to surroundings. As such, this work quantifies the radiation intensity and variation between fires from two types of LFP cell during thermal runaway. Specifically focusing on two formats, 18650 and 26650. TR was induced by constant power overheating. Heating under constant power conditions allowed the reliable and reproducible investigation of thermal runaway and flaming, while enabling the extrapolation of data relevant to real-world scenarios. All tests were carried out on fully charged cells.

Experiment observations reveal three gas emission stages: (1) steady emission post-venting, (2) increased gas production rate with self-heating, and (3) rapid gas production at thermal runaway. Forced ignition experiments sustained flames in stages 1 and 3 for 18650 cells, while 26650 cells maintained combustion throughout all stages. On average, 18650 cells resulted in a peak heat flux of approximately 150 kW/m
2
, whereas 26650 cells reached 350–500 kW/m
2
peak heat fluxes.

The spatially resolved heat flux measurements reveal significant non-uniformities in thermal radiation, with peak intensities varying by up to ∼ 10 times across the horizontal configuration. This spatial heterogeneity represents a critical finding for fire hazard assessment, as conventional single-source measurements may significantly underestimate localised heating effects on adjacent cells.

While spatial non-uniformity in radiative heat flux is common to many fire sources, the mechanisms governing non-uniformity in lithium-ion cell fires are strongly battery-specific. In our experiments, the highest radiative intensities were associated with vent-directed jet flames, changes in gas composition during different failure stages, and the intermittent release of burning ejecta. These features differ from pool or solid fuel fires, in which the flame base is diffuse and heat release is more uniformly distributed. As a result, the most severe radiative exposure during cell failure is concentrated near vent locations and along preferential flame directions, implying highly localised thermal loading on neighbouring cells and module components. This has direct consequences for thermal runaway propagation, mechanical design of battery packs, and the placement of heat-resistant barriers or sensors.

The experiments also provide insight into the flammability of cell off-gas released during failure. In all tests, venting preceding thermal runaway produced a significant quantity of combustible vapours, and ignition occurred readily when the ignition source was present. This demonstrates that off-gas generated during early venting is highly flammable and capable of supporting a sustained flame.

However, the intensity and duration of this first flame were consistently lower than those of the second flaming stage associated with thermal runaway. This indicates that the largest contribution to heat release arises from gases generated during the later decomposition of the cell materials. The transition between the two flaming stages therefore reflects a change in both gas generation rate and gas composition, leading to a marked increase in flame size and radiative output once thermal runaway is established.

An important practical implication is that flammable off-gas is present before thermal runaway is fully developed. In confined battery systems, this off-gas may accumulate and form ignitable mixtures even in the absence of visible flaming. The present results underline the need for gas management strategies that address both the flammable vent gas released prior to runaway and the more energetic gas release during runaway, as both stages contribute to fire hazard.

The quantified spatial heat fluxes provide essential data for the development and validation of computational models for predicting thermal runaway propagation in battery systems. The radiative heat flux data reported in this work are intended to provide experimentally controlled, spatially resolved measurements for single-cell fires under open conditions. While these measurements are valuable for model validation and relative hazard comparison, direct extrapolation to module- or enclosure-level scenarios should be undertaken with caution, as confinement and ventilation strongly influence flame behaviour and heat accumulation. Translation of the present results to confined or semi-confined battery systems may be achieved through coupling with enclosure-scale fire or CFD models that account for ventilation, gas accumulation, and altered radiative view factors. In practical engineering applications, the measured radiative heat fluxes may therefore be considered as baseline or lower-bound inputs.

The methodology established here enables systematic characterisation of thermal hazards at the single-cell level and provides a foundation for scaling to module and pack-level investigations. Further, these findings emphasise the dynamic hazards of off-gases, necessitating their inclusion in risk assessments. Future work, to further refine insights into battery fire hazard, will extend these measurements to larger battery formats and incorporate time-resolved gas composition analysis.

From a fire safety engineering perspective, these spatially resolved measurements have direct practical implications. First, they provide physically based inputs for assessing minimum cell spacing and evaluating the likelihood of thermal runaway propagation driven by radiative heating. Second, the directional heat flux information can inform shielding or directional protection strategies, particularly in configurations where vent orientation leads to asymmetric thermal exposure. Finally, the data provide a validation dataset for numerical fire models, which must reproduce not only global flame behaviour but also localised radiative heat transfer and spatial variability around the burning cell. Once validated, such models can be used to optimise cell spacing and design fire-resistant barriers or insulation that minimise the risk of flame spread to neighbouring cells or other combustible materials. Therefore, the principal contribution of this work lies not only in measuring radiative heat flux magnitude, but in characterising its spatial and temporal heterogeneity, which is essential for translating single-cell fire behaviour into engineering design and predictive modelling frameworks.

Francesca Lugaresi: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Project administration, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Peter Bugryniec: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Hosein Sadeghi: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Daniel Williams: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Investigation, Data curation. Mark Wootton: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Investigation, Data curation. Solomon Brown: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition. Francesco Restuccia: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.
